mnemonics.title: SELF Structure
mnemonics.content: SELF: Structure Contains Element (data), Link (pointer), Forward (to next node). Remember each node is a SELF-contained unit with forward vision.

mnemonics.title: Chain Rule
mnemonics.content: Creating linked list = CHAIN: Create node → Hook to list → Assign data → Initialize next → Navigate carefully. Break link = break chain!

mnemonics.title: Pointer Mantra
mnemonics.content: Always NULL-terminate: 'A good pointer is like a good citizen - knows when to point and when to stop (NULL)'.

mnemonics.title: NODE Operations
mnemonics.content: Insert/Delete steps: NODE - New node creation, Old link adjustment, Data assignment, Establish new links. Reverse order for deletion.

acronyms.usage: Remember all dynamic data structures start with SRS nodes
The 'next' pointer always points to another node or NULL
Four essential operations cycle in linked list management

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a box split into two: left compartment (data) + right arrow (pointer). Multiple boxes connected by arrows = linked list
Picture heap memory as a parking lot. malloc() = park a node car, free() = drive out. Pointers are parking spot numbers
Last node's pointer = 'THE END' movie title card. Missing it? You enter infinite loop hell!
Imagine flipping a chain: hold first link, reverse direction of each connection while moving down the chain

analogies.analogy: Like a treasure hunt - each clue (node) contains information (data) and directions to next clue (pointer). Lose a clue = break the chain!
Russian nesting dolls - each doll contains a smaller doll (pointer). Unlike real dolls, pointers can reference any doll in the set
Lego blocks - you can keep adding/removing blocks (nodes) anywhere. The manual (pointer variables) tracks which block comes next
Family tree where each person (node) knows their child (next pointer). But in CS, nodes can have multiple 'children' (trees/graphs)
